ai_mentions.text:
AI agents were THE hype in 2025.

But most still can't explain how they’re different from basic chatbots.

(here’s a refresher)

An AI agent is a system that can:
• Make dynamic decisions about what to do next based on what it learns
• Remember previous interactions and use that context for future decisions
• Use tools adaptively to get things done
• Change its approach when something isn't working

But not all AI agents are built the same. They exist on a spectrum based on how they're architected:

𝗦𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲-𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗦𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀

These work like intelligent routers. One agent orchestrates everything - deciding which knowledge sources to pull from, which tools to use, and how to respond. They use frameworks like ReAct (Reason + Act) to handle sequential queries while maintaining context in memory.

𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶-𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗦𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀

These are collaborative teams of specialized agents, each with distinct tools, memory, and focus areas. For example:
• One agent handling internal company data
• Another managing personal accounts and calendars
• A third searching public information via web search

By assigning clear roles, these agents can collaborate, debate, and refine outputs to solve problems that would overwhelm a single agent.

The role of vector databases? They're evolving from just knowledge sources to long-term memory systems that agents can use to extract relevant bits of prior conversations.

Learn more about AI Agents and how you can build your own reliable, enterprise-ready agents (no-code) 🧡 

Get your copy of this free ebook by @stackai and @weaviate_io:
https://t.co/COMsWkoBGG

>the repo only provided three bits of info:
- dtypes (which are always [BF16,FP4,UE8])
- config.json, which is a bit mixtral-like (gqa, swa, moe E128K4, swiglu, ntk rope)
- weights (provides numerical/shape info)

Thanks to @main_horse & @apples_jimmy

Tier List of AI Models

(for content creation)

S Tier:

- GPT 5.2 (idea generation, scripting, research)
- Claude Opus 4.6 (long-form writing, analysis, research)
- Gemini 3.1 Pro (multimodal tasks, long research, good reasoning)
- Sora 2 Pro (video generation)
- Google Veo 3.1 (video generation)
- Runway Gen-4.5 (video generation & filmmaking)
- Midjourney V7 (image generation & thumbnails)
- Suno v5 (music generation/editing)

A Tier:

- Claude Sonnet 4.6 (creative writing, long-form texts, analysis/breakdowns)
- DeepSeek V3.2 Speciale (fast reasoning, good writing)
- DeepSeek R1 (deep reasoning, research)
- Grok 4.2 (writing, browsing, research)
- Meta Llama 4 Maverick (writing styles, content repurposing)
- Mistral Large 3 (good explainers, writing optimization)
- Ideogram 3.0 (image generation, posters & ads)
- Kling 3.0 (video generation)
- ElevenLabs Multilingual v2 (voice generation)

B Tier:

- Gemini 2.5 Pro (deep research, long-form scripts, explainers)
- Qwen3.5 (multilingual content, localization, dev/technical content)
- GPT o3 (analytical takes, data breakdowns, strategic content planning)
- GPT-4o (fast ideation, threads, captions, multimodal posts)
- Sora 2 (video generation)

C Tier:

- GPT-5 mini (bulk drafting, repurposing content)
- Claude Haiku 4.5 (summaries, writing)
- Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite (quick research, multimodal tasks)
- GPT-4o mini (rewrites, generic research)

most devs using Claude Code are vibe coding with zero review process.

no architectural checks. no test coverage validation. no code smell detection before approval.

here's a plan-exit-review framework that catches everything BEFORE the agent starts writing code:

→ forces tradeoff analysis on every plan
→ enforces 1.3:1 test-to-code ratio
→ checks for DRY violations, edge cases, minimal diffs
→ catches over-engineering AND under-engineering

the gap between "AI-assisted coding" and "AI-assisted engineering" is exactly this: a review layer that thinks before it ships.
100億回言ってますが、Claudeは神です。ChatGPTでもGeminiでもない。Claude。これだけは譲れない。「どのAI使っても同じでしょ」って思ってる人、マジで損してる。全然違う。使えばわかる。今日はなぜClaudeが神なのか、具体的に話すよーん。

I spent 3.5 years finding every way AI systems break. Red teaming for OpenAI and Anthropic, capability evals at METR, automated jailbreaking, testing agents in production.

The main thing I learned: most failures aren't model failures. The model does something unexpected, sure. But the system had no review step, no recovery path, no guardrails for out-of-distribution behavior, bad context engineering, etc.

So I am building https://t.co/vxWWSpzORG. Workflow infrastructure to coordinate humans, AIs and services. Human reviews via browser extension. Failure recovery. Hybrid deployment. The goal: make the right way to build also the easiest.

Early beta. Open sourcing Q2 2026.

Lectures 13: Built Complete RAG System!
Indexing Phase:
PDF→ Chunks→ Embeddings→ Pinecone
Query Phase:
User question→ Intent Analyzer (context-aware)→ Vector search→ LLM + context
From concept to production-ready! @rohit_negi9  Bhaiya 🙏@CoderArmy 
#StrikeBatch #GenAI #RAG https://t.co/1e8Z7AWuRy

China's @Alibaba_Qwen just dropped Qwen3.5-397B under Apache 2.0 license - a massive multimodal AI that's 8.6-9x faster than previous models.

This isn't just another LLM. It's a production-ready AI agent system that understands both text and images, plans complex tasks, uses external tools, and reasons through multi-step problems.

Key capabilities:
⬩ Processes documents, charts, screenshots with human-level understanding
⬩ Writes and debugs code across 92 programming languages
⬩ Creates autonomous workflows without human intervention
⬩ Supports 201 languages with native fluency
⬩ Runs entirely on your own hardware - no API needed

The technical architecture is groundbreaking: Hybrid Linear Attention + Sparse MoE design enables it to handle 128K token contexts while maintaining blazing speed. This means it can digest entire codebases or technical manuals in seconds.

Real-world applications already emerging:
⬩ Automated customer service handling complex visual queries
⬩ Code migration between languages and frameworks
⬩ Financial analysis from raw documents and charts
⬩ Multi-step research and report generation

While the West debates regulatory frameworks, China ships open-source AI that anyone can deploy. The implications are staggering - this level of AI agent capability will automate entire departments.

If you're not building with AI or entertaining people, start learning now.

Deploy or be deployed.

🔗 https://t.co/u1XpbBw1LO

17,000 tokens per second!! Read that again!
LLM is hard-wired directly into silicon. no HBM, no liquid cooling, just raw specialized hardware. 10x faster and 20x cheaper than a B200.
the "waiting for the LLM to think" era is dead. Code generates at the speed of human thought. 
Transition from brute-force GPU clusters to actual AI appliances.
https://t.co/Bf6DH7Q6Uf

Most developers think LLM Engineering = Prompt Engineering.
That’s a costly mistake. 

If you want to build production-grade AI systems — not just demos — you need the full stack.

Here’s the real LLM Engineer roadmap 👇

1️⃣ Running LLMs – Master APIs, open-source models, prompts, and structured outputs.
2️⃣ Data Ingestion & Vector Storage – Turn messy documents into high-quality embeddings with proper chunking & hybrid search.
3️⃣ RAG – Connect models to real-world knowledge using retrievers, orchestration, memory & re-ranking.
4️⃣ Advanced RAG – Improve accuracy with query rewriting, agents, and post-processing.
5️⃣ Securing LLMs – Defend against prompt injection, backdoors, and adversarial attacks.
6️⃣ Inference Optimization – Reduce latency & cost using quantization, KV caching, Flash Attention & speculative decoding.
7️⃣ Deployment & Scaling – Move from demo to production across cloud, server, and edge.
8️⃣ Observability & Evaluation – Trace calls, monitor cost, detect hallucinations, build feedback loops.
9️⃣ Security & Guardrails – Enforce runtime safety, policy controls, and output constraints.

The shift happening right now:

AI Engineers who understand infra + optimization + security will dominate.

The rest will stay stuck at prompt level.

If you're in DevOps, backend, or cloud — this is your unfair advantage.

Which layer are you currently building in?
Drop the number (1–9) below 👇
